meta.title: Glossary
meta.intro: The domain and method vocabulary the rest of this site uses. UK energy-supply jargon, the settlement machinery, and the terms this project coined for its own architecture. Every term links to the door where you can see it working.

terms.definition: Great Britain's electricity market is settled in 48 half-hour periods per day. Every unit a supplier's customers consume is reconciled against metered volume and priced at the period's imbalance price. This simulation settles against the real Elexon half-hourly record since 2016.

The cash-out (imbalance) price a supplier pays when it is short of power in a settlement period — the price of getting the volume forecast wrong. The company prices through the realised SSP path blindfolded.
The unit of wholesale energy: one megawatt sustained for one hour. Wholesale prices are quoted in £/MWh; a typical household uses a few MWh of electricity per year.

The body that runs GB electricity settlement (the Balancing and Settlement Code). Its published half-hourly settlement data is the real-world ground truth this simulation runs against — no synthetic price path is used for the settled history.
National Energy System Operator — operator of the GB grid, publisher of demand, generation-mix and balancing data used to calibrate the simulation's world.

The set of prices at which power can be bought today for delivery in future months and seasons. A supplier hedges by buying forward; the company builds its own curve from observables and is wrong about the future by construction.
A forward curve where later delivery costs more than nearer delivery — the market pricing in expected scarcity or carrying cost. The shape of the curve drives when a supplier chooses to hedge.

The all-in cost of serving one customer — wholesale energy, network charges, policy levies, metering, billing and support. Flat margin pricing makes some customers net-negative, so any pricing model must account for cost at the customer level.
The rate at which customers leave for another supplier. Churn is a discovered quantity here: the company observes switching outcomes, it cannot read the simulation's underlying churn parameters.
Revenue less direct cost of energy, before overhead. Reported here only with its clock (see Basis) and treated strictly as a diagnostic — never a target to tune the world toward (R12).
The supplier's cash and collateral position over time — what actually lands in the bank after settlement lags and hedging margin calls, as distinct from accounting profit.
Every financial figure here carries its clock: SETTLED (accrued as energy flows), BILLED (invoiced to the customer) or BANKED (cash received). A number without its basis is a defect (R14).

The share of forecast demand a supplier has bought forward. hf=1.00 is fully hedged; hf=0.00 is fully exposed to spot. An early run converged to fully-naked hedging — the right answer for the wrong reason — which is why capital physics was added.
A statistical estimate of the worst plausible loss over a horizon at a confidence level. The company's VaR is built from its own observed history — it cannot see the simulation's risk internals.
An abrupt shift in market behaviour (e.g. the 2021–22 crisis) that breaks models calibrated on the prior calm regime. Guarded against as a known failure mode, not treated as a solved problem.

The regulator's cap on the per-unit and standing-charge tariff a supplier may charge default-tariff domestic customers, reset each quarter. A binding constraint on pricing, indexed by the date it took effect.

The machine-readable set of regulatory obligations the company must meet, each keyed by regime and carrying an effective-from / effective-to date so a rule applies only over the window it was actually in force.

Office of Gas and Electricity Markets — the GB energy regulator. Its published rules are a shared commons every layer can read; each layer's implementation of a rule stays independently owned, so a company misreading the law stays structurally possible.

The strict information boundary between the SIMULATION (ground truth) and the COMPANY (an approximation built from observables). The company can never read simulation internals; it discovers the world only the way a real supplier would. The honest gap between the two sides is the wall working, not a defect.
Every capability is a three-loop: the SIM adds depth, the COMPANY discovers and copes through the wall (allowed to be wrong), and the HARNESS measures the belief-vs-truth gap. The gap is the score.

The build backlog is a map of atoms (units of capability), each carrying a maturity level L0–L3. A level is a claim about how thoroughly a capability is built and verified, and can be demoted as easily as promoted.

A named era of the build with its own exit tests — the narrative arc of the project. Build order follows compounding return, not epoch order, so a later-epoch atom can be built first if it shortens the feedback loop.

A badge on any figure that is an estimate or forecast rather than a settled fact — for example enterprise value. Provisional figures are explicitly retractable and are labelled as such wherever they appear.

A test that deliberately breaks the thing a control checks, to prove the control actually fires (R15). A control that cannot be made to fail is worse than none — it is theatre. No promotion rests on a control that has not survived this.
The site is a rendering of repo data, never a hand-authored page. When the underlying data regenerates, the numbers on the page move with it; the page is generated, not written.

Blue / Red / Amber / Green status grammar used site-wide, where colour is information not decoration: green on-track, amber at-risk or provisional, red blocked or failing, blue verified-done or a link.
